If I ask you something you might have never heard before, can you answer it? Obviously, you would say, “Sorry, I can’t answer this question.” But what if I told you that it is completely possible? You would probably ask, “Raza, how is that possible?”
Now, let’s understand how it is possible. This is where the ChaiCode Gen AI Course comes into the picture. On 29 June 2026, we had our first class, Introduction to Gen AI. In this class, Hitesh Choudhary Sir (HC) and Piyush Garg Sir (PG) taught us a lot of interesting concepts.
Now, you might ask again, “But Raza, how can we answer a question that we have never heard before?” Let’s come to the main point.
Suppose I tell you this sequence of numbers: 10, 20, 30, 40. Then I ask you, “What is the next number?” Even though I never told you the answer, you would quickly identify the pattern and predict that the next number is 50.
Great job! You used your logical reasoning to identify the pattern and predict the next number, which is 50.Just like you identified the pattern and predicted the next number, AI also identifies patterns from what it has learned and predicts the most likely answer to your question.
LLM and Tokenization
Hey, can you tell me what 2 + 2 is? You would immediately answer 4.
Now, if I ask you, “Can ChatGPT or any other AI model calculate it?” you would probably say yes, because whenever we ask ChatGPT 2 + 2, it gives us the correct answer, which is 4.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Now, here comes the interesting part. LLMs are not actually calculating the answer in the way a calculator does. The basic principle of an LLM is prediction. Whenever we ask something to ChatGPT, our input is first converted into tokens, and the model also generates its response one token at a time. It predicts the next most likely token based on the previous tokens and the patterns it learned during training.
These LLMs are pre-trained on a massive amount of data, which is why their predictions are often very accurate.
For example, if I say to an AI, “Hello, how are you?”, it might reply, “I’m fine.” Behind the scenes, it predicts the response one token at a time. It may first predict “I”, then “‘m”, then “fine”, and so on until the complete response is generated.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A good example of this is the word “strawberry.” If you ask an LLM, “How many ‘r’s are there in the word ‘strawberry’?”, some models may answer incorrectly because they are predicting tokens rather than explicitly counting each character. Modern models have improved a lot, but this example helps us understand how LLMs fundamentally work.
As I mentioned earlier, LLMs are pre-trained. Suppose you ask, “What’s the weather in Lahore today?” A pre-trained model cannot know the latest weather on its own because it was trained on data available up to a certain point in time. If it gives you the current weather correctly, it is most likely using an external tool or searching the web to retrieve live information.
Let me prove the prediction concept with another example. If I ask you 5 + 7, you will probably answer 12 immediately without consciously performing the calculation. Why? Because you have solved similar problems so many times that your brain instantly recognizes the pattern. In a way, you have already been “trained” on this knowledge.
LLMs work in a similar manner:
Input → Tokens → Prediction → Output Tokens
Tokenization
Now, let’s talk about tokenization.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

LLMs (Larg Language Models ) and computers cannot directly understand natural languages such as English, Urdu, Hindi, or Punjabi. Instead, they process numerical representations. Before an LLM can understand our prompt, it first converts the input text into tokens, and each token is mapped to a numerical ID that the model can process.
For example:
Normal text:
“Hi, this is me Raza Ali”
Token IDs (GPT-4o tokenizer):
12194, 11, 495, 382, 668, 460, 10051, 23100
This process of converting text into tokens is called tokenization.
I generated these token IDs using the GPT-4o Token Visualizer.
We can also encode text into tokens and decode tokens back into text using the JavaScript package tiktoken. The example shown in the image demonstrates exactly how this works.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Transformers
A Transformer is the main thing behind almost every modern AI model like ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, etc.
It was introduced in 2017 in a paper called “Attention Is All You Need”. After that, AI completely changed how it understands language.
Before Transformers, AI used to:

• read sentence word by word

• forget long context easily

• give slower and weaker results
But Transformers fixed this.
Now AI can:

• look at the full sentence at once

• understand context properly

• connect related words using attention

• give better and more meaningful answers

Why it changed AI
The main reason is simple — Transformers don’t treat every word the same.
They use something called Self-Attention, which helps the model figure out which words are important in a sentence.
So instead of just matching words, it actually understands the meaning behind the sentence.
That’s why AI feels “smart” now.

How it understands language
When we type something:

• first it breaks text into tokens

• then it looks at all tokens together

• checks which words are connected

• understands the context

• then builds meaning from it
So it’s not reading like humans word by word. It processes everything together.

Why almost all LLMs use Transformers
Because:

• they work really well with large data

• they handle long text better

• they are faster and more accurate

• they scale easily
That’s why almost every LLM today (GPT, Gemini, Claude, Llama) is built on Transformers.
Simple flow
User → Prompt → Tokens → Transformer → Response
So this was the basic idea of today’s class.
We learned how ChatGPT actually works behind the scenes — from tokens, to Transformers, to how it predicts the next word step by step.
It’s not magic, it’s just patterns, math, and probability working together.
This was our first step into Gen AI, and honestly it was really interesting to see how something we use daily actually works inside.
